name: Hagia Sophia
description: A towering masterpiece of Byzantine architecture, now serving as a mosque and one of the world's most visited monuments. Its immense dome and gold mosaics leave every visitor speechless.

name: Topkapi Palace
description: The grand administrative and residential palace of the Ottoman sultans for four centuries. Its treasury houses stunning imperial jewels and sacred relics.

name: Blue Mosque
description: Sultan Ahmed Mosque, famous worldwide for its six minarets and stunning interior adorned with over 20,000 hand-painted Iznik tiles in cool blue tones.

name: Basilica Cistern
description: An underground Byzantine water reservoir supported by 336 marble columns, featuring the eerie upside-down Medusa heads at its base. A true hidden underworld of Istanbul.

name: Galata Tower
description: A medieval stone tower rising above the rooftops of Galata, offering panoramic 360-degree views of Istanbul and the Bosphorus. Built by the Genoese in 1348.

name: Dolmabahçe Palace
description: The last imperial palace of the Ottoman Empire, a European-style marvel on the Bosphorus shore boasting the world's largest Bohemian crystal chandelier. Atatürk spent his final days here.

name: Pandeli Restaurant
description: An Istanbul institution since 1901, nestled inside the Egyptian Bazaar with tiled Ottoman interiors. Famous for its lamb stew and böreks beloved by locals for generations.

name: Çiya Sofrası
description: A legendary Kadıköy restaurant preserving Anatolian village recipes that are disappearing elsewhere. The meze selection alone attracts food researchers from around the world.

name: Karaköy Güllüoğlu
description: Istanbul's most celebrated baklava house, serving paper-thin filo pastry soaked in clarified butter and pistachio since 1949. The lines are always worth it.

name: Vefa Bozacısı
description: A historic boza house open since 1876, still serving the fermented grain drink from the same marble counters. Atatürk himself was a customer — his glass is still on display.

name: Hafız Mustafa
description: One of Istanbul's oldest confectionery chains, beloved for its lokum (Turkish delight), künefe, and freshly baked simit served with clotted cream.

name: Mandabatmaz
description: A tiny, legendary coffee shop on a back alley in Beyoğlu serving the thickest, most intense Turkish coffee in the city. No table — just a stone ledge and pure coffee culture.

name: Petra Roastery
description: A specialty coffee pioneer in Istanbul's Cihangir neighborhood, roasting single-origin beans and offering expert pour-overs in a cozy, light-filled space.

name: Walter's Coffee Roastery
description: A quirky Breaking Bad–themed cafe on the Asian side of Istanbul with industrial interiors and exceptional cold brew. A cult favorite among the city's coffee aficionados.

name: Güllüoğlu Karaköy Café
description: The sit-down café annex of the famous baklava house, serving pastries alongside excellent Turkish tea and coffee with a Bosphorus glimpse.

name: Istanbul Modern
description: Turkey's first modern and contemporary art museum, housed in a striking building on the Bosphorus. It showcases landmark works by Turkish artists alongside international exhibitions.

name: Pera Museum
description: A distinguished private museum in Beyoğlu housing Orientalist paintings, Anatolian weights and measures, and the iconic Tortoise Trainer by Osman Hamdi Bey.

name: Rahmi Koç Museum
description: An extraordinary industrial museum on the Golden Horn filled with vintage cars, ships, aircraft, and steam engines — a paradise for history and technology enthusiasts.

name: Turkish & Islamic Arts Museum
description: Housed in the 16th-century İbrahim Paşa Palace, this museum holds one of the world's finest collections of Islamic calligraphy, carpets, and ethnographic objects.

name: Grand Bazaar
description: One of the world's oldest and largest covered markets, with over 4,000 shops selling carpets, jewelry, ceramics, and spices across 61 covered streets. A sensory labyrinth.

name: Egyptian Bazaar (Spice Bazaar)
description: A vibrant 17th-century market overflowing with spices, dried fruits, nuts, Turkish delight, and herbal teas. The aromas alone make it unmissable.

name: Arasta Bazaar
description: A quieter, more authentic alternative to the Grand Bazaar, steps from the Blue Mosque. Specializes in quality handmade ceramics, kilims, and Ottoman-style souvenirs.

name: Balat
description: A UNESCO-listed historic neighborhood of colorful crumbling houses, centuries-old synagogues, and hip cafes. Once home to Greek and Jewish communities, now a bohemian enclave.

name: Kuzguncuk
description: A charming Bosphorus village on the Asian shore where wooden houses painted in pastel colors line cobblestone streets. Beloved by artists and writers escaping the city's chaos.

name: Ortaköy Sahili
description: A scenic Bosphorus waterfront square dominated by the 19th-century Ortaköy Mosque and the Bosphorus Bridge. Famous for its waffle stalls and weekend craft markets.

name: Aşiyan Cemetery
description: A romantic hilltop cemetery above the Bosphorus village of Rumelihisarı, final resting place of Ottoman poets and intellectuals. Hauntingly beautiful and almost unknown.

name: Bosphorus Walk
description: A scenic waterfront promenade stretching along the European shore of the Bosphorus from Beşiktaş to Ortaköy. Spectacular views of Dolmabahçe Palace and Asian hills.

name: Balat–Fener Walk
description: A walking route through Istanbul's oldest Christian and Jewish neighborhoods, passing Byzantine churches, Ottoman fountains, and stairways draped in colorful laundry.

name: Prince Islands Trail
description: A bicycle and carriage trail circling Büyükada, the largest of Istanbul's car-free Princes Islands. Pine forests, belle époque villas, and sea views at every turn.

name: Belgrad Forest
description: Istanbul's green lung — a vast oak and beech forest north of the city with cycling paths, Ottoman aqueducts, and reservoirs. Perfect for weekend escapes from urban life.

name: Emirgan Park
description: Istanbul's most beloved public park on the Bosphorus, home to three 19th-century wooden kiosks and famous for its explosive tulip displays every April.

name: Princes Islands
description: A chain of nine car-free islands in the Sea of Marmara, reachable by ferry from the city. Pine-scented walks, pebble beaches, and a welcome escape from Istanbul's density.

name: Babylon Istanbul
description: Istanbul's most respected live music venue, hosting international and Turkish artists across jazz, rock, and electronic genres. A cornerstone of the city's nightlife since 1999.

name: Bant Mag Bahçe
description: An open-air bar and music garden in Karaköy run by the Turkish music magazine Bant Mag. Attracts the city's indie crowd with eclectic programming and laid-back garden vibes.

name: Arkaoda
description: A cult Kadıköy venue spreading across multiple floors with live music, DJs, a bookshop, and rooftop terrace. The heartbeat of Istanbul's alternative nightlife scene.

name: Agora of Smyrna
description: Remarkably preserved ruins of the ancient Roman agora lying right in the heart of modern Izmir. Stoa colonnades, vaulted arcades, and open-air excavation pits make it a must.

name: Kemeraltı Bazaar
description: Izmir's ancient bazaar stretching through a labyrinth of caravanserais, mosques, and covered passages. A living museum of trade where vendors sell herbs, fabrics, and antiques.

name: Kordon Promenade
description: Izmir's beloved seafront promenade lined with palm trees and historic buildings, stretching along the Aegean shore. Best enjoyed at sunset with a glass of Aegean wine.

name: Kadifekale
description: The ancient Velvet Castle perched on a hill above Izmir with sweeping views of the bay. Built by Lysimachos, the Hellenistic walls still stand among scattered wildflowers.

name: Alsancak Gastronomy Strip
description: Izmir's most vibrant dining street lined with Aegean mezze houses, fresh seafood restaurants, and cocktail bars. The city's equivalent of a great food boulevard.

name: Boyoz at Sevinç Pastanesi
description: The best place in Izmir to taste boyoz, the flaky Sephardic pastry unique to the city. This historic bakery has been perfecting the recipe since the 1950s.

name: Ephesus Ancient City
description: One of the best-preserved ancient cities in the Mediterranean, with the Library of Celsus, marble-paved streets, and a theatre seating 25,000. A day trip from Izmir.

name: Çeşme Beaches
description: Crystal-clear Aegean coves and windsurfing beaches just outside Izmir. Ilıca and Altınkum beaches offer turquoise shallows and thermal springs right at the shore.

name: Izmir Archaeology Museum
description: Houses thousands of artifacts from excavations across the Aegean region including the famous Apollo and Demeter statues from the Agora. A compact but world-class collection.

name: Kaleiçi Old Quarter
description: Antalya's charming walled old city, a maze of Roman, Byzantine, and Ottoman layers with marble alleys, restored mansions, and restaurants set in ancient courtyards.

name: Hadrian's Gate
description: A magnificent triumphal arch built in 130 AD to honor Emperor Hadrian's visit to Antalya. Three arched marble gateways frame the entrance to the old city with startling grace.

name: Düden Waterfall
description: A dramatic waterfall where the Düden River plunges directly into the Mediterranean Sea. The viewing platform lets you stand behind the curtain of falling water.

name: Konyaaltı Beach
description: A long pebble beach stretching beneath the Taurus Mountains with crystal Mediterranean waters. Backed by a modern park with bike paths, beach clubs, and the city skyline.

name: Antalya Archaeological Museum
description: One of Turkey's finest museums, with halls of Roman-era sarcophagi, the magnificent Gallery of the Gods, and treasures from Perge and Aspendos.

name: Perge Ancient City
description: A remarkably intact Greco-Roman city just east of Antalya, featuring colonnaded avenues, a theatre, and a stadium that once held 12,000 spectators.

name: Liman Lokantası
description: A historic workers' canteen by the harbor that has been serving Antalya fishermen and locals for decades with honest Anatolian food at budget prices.

name: Lycian Way Coastal Trail
description: A section of Turkey's famous long-distance hiking path following clifftop trails above the turquoise Mediterranean, passing Lycian rock tombs and hidden bays.

name: Mermerli Café & Beach
description: A secret café and tiny private beach hidden below Kaleiçi's walls, accessible through a tunnel. Known only to locals who prize it for sunset cocktails over calm water.

name: Colosseum
description: The colossal Flavian amphitheatre, capable of holding 80,000 spectators, where gladiators fought and emperors staged elaborate public spectacles. A timeless symbol of Rome.

name: Vatican Museums
description: A vast complex of papal collections including the Sistine Chapel, the Gallery of Maps, and Raphael Rooms. Over 54 galleries hold one of the world's greatest art treasures.

name: Trevi Fountain
description: Rome's grandest Baroque fountain, where Neptune commands the waters at the junction of three ancient aqueducts. Toss a coin and you are guaranteed to return to Rome.

name: Pantheon
description: The best-preserved ancient Roman building, with its unreinforced concrete dome and 9-metre oculus open to the sky. Still standing after nearly 2,000 years.

name: Roman Forum & Palatine Hill
description: The beating heart of ancient Rome, where Julius Caesar was assassinated, Mark Antony gave his eulogy, and emperors lived in vast hilltop palaces above the city.

name: Trastevere Trattorias
description: Rome's most atmospheric neighbourhood for dining, with ivy-draped trattorias serving cacio e pepe, carbonara, and crispy supplì in candlelit cobblestone alleys.

name: Roscioli Salumeria con Cucina
description: A legendary Roman deli-restaurant that is simultaneously a butcher, cheese shop, wine cellar, and restaurant. The carbonara is considered by many the best in Rome.

name: Sant'Eustachio il Caffè
description: Perhaps Rome's most revered espresso bar, roasting beans the same way since 1938. The gran caffè with a secret recipe has been drawing pilgrims for generations.

name: Testaccio Market
description: Rome's most authentic covered food market, where chefs and Roman families buy produce, offal, cheese, and street food. The suppli stall is unmissable.

name: Borghese Gallery
description: Arguably the finest small museum in the world, housing Bernini's breathtaking sculptures and Caravaggio masterpieces in a 17th-century villa surrounded by a magnificent park.

name: Via Appia Antica
description: Walk the ancient cobblestones of the 312 BC Appian Way, lined with crumbling Roman tombs, catacombs, and pastoral views unchanged since antiquity.

name: Pigneto Neighbourhood
description: Rome's coolest working-class neighbourhood, the setting of Pasolini films and now home to aperitivo bars, vinyl record shops, and street art. The real Rome away from tourists.

name: Villa Borghese Gardens
description: Rome's most beloved public park, 80 hectares of landscape gardens, fountains, and pine trees in the heart of the city. Rent a rowboat, cycle, or simply picnic with Romans.

name: Eiffel Tower
description: Gustave Eiffel's 330-metre iron lattice masterpiece, built for the 1889 World's Fair and now the world's most visited monument. Breathtaking from any angle, magical at night.

name: The Louvre
description: The world's largest art museum, housing over 35,000 works including the Mona Lisa, Venus de Milo, and Winged Victory. A former royal palace whose glass pyramid is iconic.

name: Notre-Dame Cathedral
description: The Gothic masterpiece of medieval Paris, whose restoration after the 2019 fire is now complete. A forest of flying buttresses, gargoyles, and the most famous rose windows in the world.

name: Musée d'Orsay
description: A converted Beaux-Arts railway station housing the world's greatest collection of Impressionist art — Monet's Water Lilies, Renoir, Van Gogh, Degas, and more.

name: Montmartre & Sacré-Cœur
description: Paris's hilltop bohemian village, crowned by the white-domed Sacré-Cœur basilica. Wander vine-covered lanes where Picasso, Toulouse-Lautrec, and Renoir once lived.

name: Le Comptoir du Relais
description: The most coveted brasserie table in Paris, run by legendary chef Yves Camdeborde serving exquisite bistronomy in the heart of Saint-Germain-des-Prés.

name: Septime
description: Paris's most-booked natural wine bistro, serving market-driven bistronomie in a relaxed industrial setting in Bastille. Reservations required weeks in advance.

name: Café de Flore
description: Paris's most legendary café, where Sartre, de Beauvoir, and Picasso wrote and debated over café crème. Still serving exceptional croissants on Boulevard Saint-Germain.

name: Ten Belles
description: The coffee shop that launched Paris's specialty coffee revolution, serving Scandinavian-roasted single origins on the Canal Saint-Martin with avocado toasts and excellent pastries.

name: Marché Bastille
description: Paris's most beloved outdoor market on Boulevard Richard-Lenoir, running every Thursday and Sunday. Locals shop for seasonal vegetables, charcuterie, and artisan cheeses.

name: Canal Saint-Martin Walk
description: A picturesque canal walk through Paris's trendiest neighbourhood, crossing iron footbridges and passing bateaux-lavoirs where locals picnic on the tree-lined embankments.

name: Bois de Boulogne
description: A vast 845-hectare forest park on the western edge of Paris with lakes, cycling paths, the Fondation Louis Vuitton museum, and the Roland-Garros tennis grounds.

name: Passage des Panoramas
description: Paris's oldest covered arcade, built in 1799 and still lined with stamp dealers, vintage print shops, and tiny restaurants. A hidden world untouched by mass tourism.

name: Rex Club
description: Paris's most iconic techno club, running in the basement of the Grand Rex cinema since 1981. A no-frills, dark room with an extraordinary sound system attracting the world's best DJs.

name: Senso-ji Temple
description: Tokyo's oldest and most iconic Buddhist temple, founded in 628 AD in Asakusa. The Kaminarimon Thunder Gate and Nakamise shopping street lead to a complex of stunning pagodas and incense smoke.

name: Meiji Jingu Shrine
description: A vast Shinto shrine in a dense 70-hectare forest dedicated to Emperor Meiji and Empress Shoken. The towering torii gate marks the transition from the city's noise to forest silence.

name: Imperial Palace East Gardens
description: The former innermost enclosure of Edo Castle, home of the Tokugawa Shoguns. Immaculately kept traditional gardens with stone tower ruins and viewing pavilions set among seasonal plum and cherry blossoms.

name: Ueno Toshogu Shrine
description: A lavishly gilded Edo-period shrine inside Ueno Park, one of the few original structures surviving from the 1600s. Its golden lacquer facade and stone lanterns are extraordinary.

name: Tsukiji Outer Market
description: The outer ring of Tokyo's legendary fish market still thrives with sushi bars, fresh seafood stands, and tamagoyaki egg vendors. Arriving at 7am for the freshest omakase is a rite of passage.

name: Ichiran Ramen
description: Japan's most celebrated solo dining experience — individual booth partitions, no eye contact, a personal order form, and a perfect bowl of tonkotsu ramen adjusted exactly to your preferences.

name: Sukiyabashi Jiro
description: Three Michelin stars in a Ginza subway concourse. Jiro Ono, born 1925, crafts each piece with lifelong precision. Obama ate here. The 20-course omakase takes 30 minutes and costs 40,000 yen.

name: Tempura Kondo
description: Fumio Kondo's two-Michelin-star Ginza restaurant where vegetable tempura is elevated to high art. His carrot and sweet potato pieces have the lightest batter in the world.

name: Tokyo National Museum
description: Japan's largest and oldest museum in Ueno Park, holding 110,000 objects including the world's largest collection of Japanese art — samurai armour, ukiyo-e prints, Buddhist sculpture, and national treasures.

name: teamLab Planets
description: An immersive digital art museum in Toyosu where you wade through knee-deep water reflecting infinite flower projections. Four giant rooms dissolve the boundary between your body and the artwork.

name: Mori Art Museum
description: A world-class contemporary art museum on the 53rd floor of Roppongi Hills Tower with floor-to-ceiling city views. Rotating exhibitions of top global and Japanese contemporary artists.

name: Golden Gai
description: A labyrinth of 200 tiny wooden bars crammed into six narrow alleys in Shinjuku. Each bar has room for 5-8 people and a distinct personality. This is Tokyo's most atmospheric drinking neighborhood.

name: Bar High Five
description: Hidetsugu Ueno's legendary Ginza cocktail bar ranked among Asia's 50 Best. No menu — describe a mood or flavor and the bartender crafts a bespoke cocktail of extraordinary precision.

name: Robot Restaurant
description: A gloriously absurd neon-lit show in Shinjuku featuring giant robots, lasers, taiko drummers, and dancers in a basement that cost 10 billion yen to decorate. Pure Tokyo spectacle.

name: Shinjuku Gyoen National Garden
description: Tokyo's finest garden spanning 58 hectares with French formal, English landscape, and Japanese traditional sections. The 1,100 cherry trees draw enormous crowds each March and April.

name: Mount Takao
description: A sacred 599m mountain an hour from Shinjuku with cedar forests, free-roaming monkeys, and ancient Yakuoin temple. One of the world's most-climbed mountains yet always feels serene.

name: Yoyogi Park
description: A vast green oasis next to Harajuku where Tokyoites practice parkour, strum guitars, do tai chi, and walk dogs on Sunday mornings. The adjacent Meiji Shrine forest makes this feel far from the city.

name: % Arabica Roppongi
description: The Tokyo outpost of Kyoto's minimalist specialty coffee brand. White walls, an immaculate La Marzocca machine, and some of the cleanest espresso in Asia. The matcha latte is a ritual.

name: Fuglen Tokyo
description: An Oslo café transplanted to Tomigaya with vintage 1960s Scandinavian furniture. Coffee by day, natural wine and cocktails by night. The specialty pour-overs attract Tokyo's coffee cognoscenti.

name: Koffee Mameya
description: A hushed consultation coffee shop in Omotesando where white-coated baristas guide you through bean selections like sommeliers. Only 8 stools, no espresso machine — pour-over only.

name: Nakameguro Canal Walk
description: A 2km canalside promenade lined with cherry trees, indie boutiques, and cafés beneath. In spring, the overhanging sakura form a pink tunnel over the water that attracts the entire city.

name: Yanaka Ginza
description: A preserved old shopping street where Tokyo's Shitamachi spirit survives: tofu shops, grilled skewer stalls, and cats sleeping in the sun. One of the few neighborhoods untouched by WWII bombs.

name: Ameyoko Market
description: A chaotic open-air market under the Ueno elevated train tracks that began as a black market after WWII. Today: dried seafood, cosmetics, street food, and vendors shouting prices at full volume.

name: Shimokitazawa
description: Tokyo's hippest neighborhood with hundreds of vintage clothing stores, small live music venues, indie theaters, and zero chain stores. The antithesis of Shibuya, and far more interesting.

name: Kagurazaka
description: A cobblestone hillside with French-Japanese fusion identity — geisha alleys alongside French bakeries, a Michelin-starred French restaurant next to a Buddhist temple. One of Tokyo's most elegant surprises.

name: Sagrada Família
description: Gaudí's unfinished basilica and the world's most visited construction site. Under continuous construction since 1882, its organic stone towers and kaleidoscopic stained glass windows are unlike any building on earth.

name: Barri Gòtic
description: Barcelona's medieval labyrinth built over Roman ruins — a 2,000-year-old Temple of Augustus stands in a courtyard most tourists walk past. Every narrow alley reveals Roman columns, Gothic archways, and gargoyles.

name: Park Güell
description: Gaudí's mosaic-covered hillside garden with the famous dragon staircase, serpentine bench with city views, and the Monumental Zone where no straight line exists. Built 1900–1914 for a housing estate that never happened.

name: Palau de la Música Catalana
description: Domènech i Montaner's staggering UNESCO concert hall built 1905–08. The interior is a forest of stained glass, ceramic roses, and a centrepiece skylight that floods the stage in color. Still hosts concerts weekly.

name: La Boqueria
description: Barcelona's legendary covered market since 1840, crammed with jamón legs, whole octopus, fruit stalls, and overflowing seafood counters. The counter bar at Pinotxo has served breakfast to locals since 1940.

name: Bodega 1900
description: Albert Adrià's (El Bulli) love letter to the old-fashioned Barcelona bodega. Vermouth on tap, modernist canned food elevated to haute cuisine, and the finest patatas bravas in the city.

name: Bar del Pla
description: A neighbourhood institution in El Born with legendary croquetas de jamón, escalivada, and an extensive sherry list. Marble tables, a zinc bar, and a mix of locals and those in the know.

name: Museu Picasso
description: Over 4,000 Picasso works in five interconnected medieval palaces in El Born. The collection's strength is his early formative years in Barcelona — long before Cubism — revealing the prodigy behind the genius.

name: MNAC – Museu Nacional d'Art de Catalunya
description: A Romanesque art collection without equal, housed in the hilltop Palau Nacional. The medieval frescoes rescued from Pyrenean churches are staggering; the rooftop offers the best panorama of Barcelona.

name: Fundació Joan Miró
description: Sert's brilliant white building on Montjuïc holds 10,000 Miró works — paintings, sculptures, and tapestries in primary colors that feel joyful even in their abstraction. His studio is preserved as he left it.

name: Razzmatazz
description: Five rooms, five different musical styles from indie to techno in a former industrial warehouse in Poblenou. Headline acts play the main Razz Club while ravers fill the smaller rooms until 6am.

name: Bar Marsella
description: Barcelona's oldest bar, open since 1820 in El Raval. Dusty bottles of absinthe hang from a ceiling unchanged for two centuries. Hemingway drank here. The absinthe is still served with a lump of sugar and a flame.

name: El Born Cocktail Strip
description: The trendiest cocktail corridor in the old quarter — El Xampanyet, Bar del Convent, and a dozen craft bars on Carrer del Rec and Passeig del Born. An outdoor terrace culture fueled by natural wine and vermouth.

name: Montjuïc Hill
description: A forested hill rising 184m above the port with botanical gardens, a castle, the 1992 Olympic stadium, and Miró foundation. The cable car from Barceloneta gives a dramatic arrival over the sea.

name: Tibidabo Mountain
description: Barcelona's highest point at 512m, crowned with a century-old amusement park and the Sacred Heart basilica whose spire is visible from anywhere in the city. On clear days you can see Mallorca.

name: Barceloneta Beach
description: The city's main urban beach where locals swim, play pétanque, and feast on seafood in the chiringuitos. The waterfront from Port Olímpic to Barceloneta is a 4km stretch of Mediterranean leisure.

name: Las Ramblas
description: Barcelona's legendary tree-lined boulevard running 1.2km from Plaça de Catalunya to the sea, past the Boqueria, living statues, flower stalls, and the Columbus Monument at its southern end.

name: Eixample Modernisme Walk
description: A self-guided walk through Ildefons Cerdà's 19th-century grid to spot Gaudí's Casa Batlló, Casa Milà, Domènech's Casa Lleó Morera, and Puig's Casa Terrades — five Modernisme masterpieces in six city blocks.

name: Bunkers del Carmel
description: The ruins of a Civil War anti-aircraft battery on a hilltop in Gràcia with the best 360-degree panorama in Barcelona. Locals bring wine, blankets, and guitars for sunset. Completely free and never crowded.

name: Rambla del Poblenou
description: A quiet local Rambla in Barcelona's former industrial district, now filled with art studios, craft beer bars, and ceramics workshops. The opposite of the tourist Ramblas — where Barcelona actually lives.

name: Mercat de l'Abaceria
description: Gràcia's beloved 1892 iron-frame market selling organic produce, spices, vintage clothes, and antiques alongside food stalls serving vermouth and croquetas to local families on Saturday mornings.

name: Encants Barcelona
description: Europe's oldest flea market, now under a spectacular mirrored steel canopy near Glòries. Second-hand furniture, vinyl records, antique ceramics, and used books spread over 8,000 square metres.

name: Brandenburg Gate
description: An 18th-century neoclassical triumphal arch and the enduring symbol of German reunification. Napoleon marched through it, the Wall was built beside it, and half a million people gathered here in 1989.

name: Berlin Wall Memorial
description: A preserved 80m section of the Wall on Bernauer Strasse with a documentation center, outdoor exhibition, and the ghost of the death strip between the two parallel walls that ran through a city for 28 years.

name: Reichstag & Glass Dome
description: The German parliament topped by Norman Foster's iconic glass cupola that visitors walk through for free. Panoramic city views, ramps spiraling to the apex, and the powerful symbolism of transparency in democracy.

name: Holocaust Memorial
description: Peter Eisenman's field of 2,711 concrete stelae of varying heights creating a labyrinthine space where daylight and disorientation converge. Below lies an information center with individual stories of victims.

name: Pergamon Museum
description: Germany's most-visited museum on Museum Island holding the massive Pergamon Altar (180 BC) and the Ishtar Gate of Babylon (575 BC). Entire ancient facades were dismantled and rebuilt inside these halls.

name: Neues Museum
description: David Chipperfield's masterful restoration of a WWII-bombed 1855 museum now holds the Egyptian collection including the famous Nefertiti bust. The deliberately preserved bomb damage makes history visible in the walls.

name: East Side Gallery
description: The longest surviving stretch of the Berlin Wall — 1.3km covered in murals by 105 artists from 21 countries immediately after reunification in 1990. Dmitri Vrubel's Fraternal Kiss is the most photographed image.

name: Gemäldegalerie
description: One of the world's great collections of Old Masters — 72 rooms of Raphael, Rembrandt, Vermeer, Caravaggio, and Botticelli. Unlike Paris or London, there are rarely queues. The Raffael hall alone is worth the visit.

name: Berghain
description: The world's most famous techno club in a former East Berlin power plant. The door policy is a ritual of rejection that makes entry feel like an achievement. No phones allowed. The music doesn't stop for 48 hours.

name: Tresor Club
description: Berlin's original techno institution since 1991, first in a bank vault, now in a former power plant near Potsdamer Platz. Three floors, relentless industrial techno, and the iconic low-ceiling vault room.

name: Watergate
description: A Kreuzberg club floating over the Spree with floor-to-ceiling water-view windows and a terrace directly above the river. Hosts the finest house and techno lineups with an intimate crowd.

name: Curry 36
description: The Kreuzberg currywurst institution since 1980 where construction workers and politicians line up together at the outdoor counter. Grilled pork sausage, a Heinz ketchup and curry powder sauce, and a bread roll.

name: Mustafa's Gemüse Kebab
description: A legendary Kreuzberg döner stand with a permanent queue. The Gemüse Kebab is Berlin's greatest contribution to street food — grilled halloumi, roasted vegetables, and herb sauces stuffed into lavash bread.

name: Nobelhart & Schmutzig
description: Berlin's most radical restaurant: a 28-seat counter where only ingredients from Brandenburg state are used. A political act disguised as a tasting menu. No imports, no olive oil. Only what grows here.

name: Unter den Linden
description: Berlin's grand ceremonial boulevard lined with linden trees, running from Brandenburg Gate to the palace bridge and Museum Island. Prussian grandeur, state opera, Humboldt university, and the heart of old Berlin.

name: Mauerpark Flea Market
description: A vast Sunday flea market on the former Wall strip in Prenzlauer Berg. 200+ vendors selling vintage clothes, vinyl, antique furniture, Soviet memorabilia, and street food. The Sunday karaoke amphitheater is mandatory.

name: Boxhagener Platz Market
description: Friedrichshain's neighborhood Saturday market where organic vegetables, artisan bread, and coffee from local roasters fill the square. The Sunday flea market with vintage books and furniture attracts the entire Kiez.

name: Tempelhofer Feld
description: A decommissioned airport runway turned into an enormous urban park where Berliners cycle, rollerblade, fly kites, grow community gardens, and watch sunset over the flat horizon. A city that turned an airport into freedom.

name: Klunkerkranich
description: A rooftop bar on top of a Neukölln shopping centre that has grown into a wild urban garden with climbing plants, wooden decks, and panoramic views. Free entry before 6pm. Come for the sunset, stay for the DJs.

name: Tiergarten Park
description: Berlin's vast central park — 210 hectares of forest, meadows, and canals in the heart of the city. Former royal hunting ground, now a place where Berliners grill, sunbathe nude, and boat on Sunday afternoons.

name: Grunewald Forest & Teufelsberg
description: A 3,000-hectare forest on Berlin's western edge with lakes for swimming and Teufelsberg — a Cold War NSA spy station built on rubble, now covered in street art and open for guided tours.

name: Göreme Open Air Museum
description: A UNESCO World Heritage site containing rock-cut churches adorned with Byzantine frescoes dating back to the 10th century. The Karanlık Kilise (Dark Church) has the best-preserved paintings.

name: Uçhisar Castle
description: The highest point in Cappadocia, this massive volcanic rock was hollowed out by ancient inhabitants to create a fortress-city. The views across fairy chimneys at sunset are unmatched.

name: Derinkuyu Underground City
description: An ancient multi-level underground city extending 85 meters deep, capable of sheltering tens of thousands of people. One of the most remarkable archaeological discoveries in the region.

name: Fairy Chimneys Valley
description: The iconic volcanic rock formations of Cappadocia, sculpted over millions of years into mushroom-like pillars. Pasabag Valley has the most photogenic specimens with unique multi-headed caps.

name: Red Valley Hike
description: A breathtaking hike through red and rose-hued valleys carved by volcanic activity. The trail winds past carved chapels, pigeon houses, and vineyards with dramatic views of the landscape.

name: Hot Air Balloon Sunrise
description: Drifting over the lunar landscape of Cappadocia at sunrise in a hot air balloon is one of the world's most extraordinary travel experiences. The golden light on the fairy chimneys is magical.

name: Rose Valley Trail
description: A 3-hour scenic walking route connecting Cavusin to Goreme through pink-tinted rock formations, cave churches, and ancient orchards. Best done in the late afternoon light.

name: Avanos Pottery Workshops
description: Avanos has been a center of pottery-making for over 3,000 years, using clay from the Red River. You can watch master craftspeople throw pots and take hands-on workshops.

name: Cappadocian Wine Tasting
description: The volcanic soil of Cappadocia produces exceptional wines — particularly Emir white and Okuzgozu red. Many local estates offer cave-cellar tastings among ancient carved chambers.

name: Kaymakli Underground City
description: Another vast underground settlement carved into volcanic tuff, featuring 8 excavated levels used as stables, churches, wineries, and living quarters by early Christians fleeing persecution.

name: Zelve Open Air Museum
description: A fascinating abandoned village where three valleys meet, featuring cave dwellings, churches, and monasteries carved into the rock. More rustic and less visited than Goreme.

name: Bodrum Castle (St. Peter)
description: A magnificent 15th-century Crusader castle built using stones from the Mausoleum at Halicarnassus. It houses the world's greatest underwater archaeology museum with ancient shipwrecks.

name: Mausoleum at Halicarnassus
description: The ruins of one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World, built for King Mausolus in the 4th century BC. The site museum contains recovered sculptures and architectural fragments.

name: Bitez Beach
description: A relaxed, palm-fringed bay popular with windsurfers thanks to consistent Meltemi winds. The clear turquoise water and laid-back atmosphere make it the best family beach near Bodrum.

name: Camel Beach
description: Yakaköy Cove, better known as Camel Beach, is a hidden pebble bay accessible only by boat or a steep hike. The crystal-clear water and the absence of crowds make it a paradise.

name: Gümbet Nightlife Strip
description: Bodrum's liveliest nightlife area, with beach clubs, rooftop bars, and open-air venues drawing crowds from across Turkey. The party runs from sunset to sunrise.

name: Marina Yacht Club
description: Dining along the Bodrum marina with hundreds of gleaming yachts as your backdrop. The seafood mezze and fresh fish restaurants here set the standard for Aegean cuisine.

name: Yalıkavak Village
description: An upscale fishing village on the peninsula's north tip, transformed into Bodrum's most elegant destination. The Friday market, boutique marinas, and restaurant row are unmissable.

name: Gümüşlük Village & Taverns
description: A protected archaeological zone where ancient Myndos lies beneath the sea. Walk across the sunken causeway to Rabbit Island, then dine at the fish taverns as the sun sets over Greece.

name: St. Mark's Basilica
description: One of the finest examples of Italo-Byzantine architecture, adorned with thousands of square meters of golden mosaics. The Pala d'Oro altar screen alone contains 1,927 precious stones.

name: Doge's Palace
description: The Gothic masterpiece that served as the seat of Venetian power for over 500 years. The Bridge of Sighs, the Great Council Chamber with its enormous Tintoretto, and the dungeons are unmissable.

name: Rialto Bridge & Market
description: The oldest and most iconic of Venice's four bridges over the Grand Canal, flanked by covered walkways of goldsmiths and jewelers. The adjacent Rialto Market has supplied Venetian kitchens for 1,000 years.

name: Gallerie dell'Accademia
description: The greatest collection of Venetian art anywhere, spanning eight centuries of painting from Bellini to Titian, Tintoretto, and Veronese. The Leonardo Vitruvian Man is displayed here.

name: Peggy Guggenheim Collection
description: One of Europe's most important modern art collections, housed in Peggy Guggenheim's palazzo on the Grand Canal. Works by Pollock, Picasso, Dalí, and Calder overlook the water.

name: Grand Canal by Gondola
description: Gliding silently through the Grand Canal on a gondola while a gondolier sings is the quintessential Venetian experience. Best at dusk when the palazzos are illuminated.

name: Dorsoduro Neighbourhood Walk
description: Venice's most liveable sestiere, home to Ca' Rezzonico, Campo Santa Margherita (the city's liveliest square), and the Zattere promenade with views to the Giudecca island.

name: Cicchetti Bar-Hopping
description: Venice's answer to tapas — bite-sized snacks eaten standing at bacaro wine bars. Sarde in saor, baccalà mantecato on white polenta, and tiny fried artichokes washed down with Aperol spritz.

name: Caffè Florian
description: The world's oldest continuously operating coffee house, founded in 1720 on St. Mark's Square. The frescoed rooms and live chamber music make a single espresso an unforgettable event.

name: Burano Island
description: A 40-minute vaporetto ride from Venice, Burano is famous for its candy-coloured fishermen's houses and delicate needle-lace tradition. The leaning campanile and quiet canals feel worlds away.

name: Rialto Market at Dawn
description: The wholesale fish and vegetable market that opens at 5am when Venetian chefs shop for the day's ingredients. The spectacle of cuttlefish, lagoon crabs, and razor clams is extraordinary.

name: Uffizi Gallery
description: One of the world's most important art museums, housing masterpieces from Botticelli's Birth of Venus and Primavera to Leonardo, Michelangelo, Raphael, and Caravaggio. Advance booking is essential.

name: Accademia & Michelangelo's David
description: The original David stands 5.17 meters tall in a specially designed rotunda. Seeing it in person — the determined expression, the perfect proportions — is a defining cultural experience.

name: Florence Cathedral & Dome
description: Brunelleschi's dome was the engineering marvel of the Renaissance — still the largest brick dome ever built. Climbing to the top offers unparalleled views over the red terracotta roofscape.

name: Pitti Palace
description: The largest palazzo in Florence, once the Medici family residence, now housing seven museums including the Palatine Gallery with its extraordinary concentration of Raphael and Titian paintings.

name: Boboli Gardens
description: The Renaissance garden behind Pitti Palace is one of Italy's finest examples of the art form, featuring grottos, fountains, sculptures, and an amphitheatre with panoramic views over Florence.

name: Ponte Vecchio at Dusk
description: The only Florentine bridge to survive WWII, lined with goldsmiths and jewellers since the 16th century. The view from the Arno at dusk — with the bridge lit gold — is Florence's most romantic image.

name: Oltrarno Trattorias
description: The artisan quarter across the Arno preserves old Florence: leather workshops, antique restorers, and trattorias serving ribollita, lampredotto (tripe sandwich), and bistecca Fiorentina.

name: Sant'Ambrogio Market
description: Florence's most authentic neighbourhood market, far less touristy than the central market. Local housewives shop for seasonal produce, aged cheeses, and fresh pasta alongside the restaurants.

name: Caffè Gilli
description: The oldest café in Florence, operating on Piazza della Repubblica since 1733. The Art Nouveau interior, exquisite pastries, and standing-room espresso bar make it a Florentine institution.

name: Piazzale Michelangelo
description: The hilltop terrace offering the most celebrated panorama of Florence — the Duomo, Palazzo Vecchio, and Arno river all in a single sweep. Iconic at golden hour.

name: Bardini Gardens
description: Less visited than Boboli, these terraced gardens offer arguably better views of Florence's skyline through wisteria tunnels and beneath centuries-old cedar trees.

name: San Miniato al Monte
description: A stunning Romanesque church perched above the Arno, older than the Duomo and far more serene. The monks chant vespers at 5:30pm — hearing Gregorian chant in this 1,000-year-old space is unforgettable.

name: Promenade des Anglais
description: The legendary 7km seafront promenade where Nice's Belle Époque elegance meets the azure Mediterranean. The pebble beaches, painted parasols, and Baroque facades create an irresistible scene.

name: Vieux-Nice (Old Town)
description: A labyrinth of narrow Baroque streets painted in ochre, sienna, and russet. The morning flower market on Cours Saleya, baroque churches, and Niçoise cuisine make it the soul of the city.

name: Colline du Château
description: The promontory between Nice's two ports offers the most spectacular viewpoint over the Baie des Anges and the rooftops of the old town. The 92m climb (or lift) is absolutely worth it.

name: Cours Saleya Flower Market
description: One of France's most famous markets, held every morning except Monday. Stalls overflow with Provençal flowers, socca, local olives, and the freshest produce from the surrounding hills.

name: Musée Matisse
description: Henri Matisse spent much of his life in Nice — this beautiful Genoese villa in the olive groves of Cimiez houses the world's finest collection of his paintings, drawings, and sculptures.

name: Musée National Chagall
description: A purpose-built museum designed by Chagall himself to display his monumental Biblical Message series — 17 huge canvases in vivid blues and golds in a garden setting.

name: Socca at Lou Pilha Leva
description: Socca — a thick chickpea flour pancake cooked in a wood-fired oven — is Nice's defining street food. Lou Pilha Leva in the old town has served the best version for generations.

name: Villefranche-sur-Mer
description: A perfect fishing village 6km from Nice with a deep natural harbour and a citadel protecting colourful houses stacked above the sea. The Chapel of St. Peter, decorated by Jean Cocteau, is a gem.

name: Vieux Lyon (Old Town)
description: The largest Renaissance district in France, a UNESCO World Heritage site. The maze of traboules (secret passageways through buildings) once used by silk merchants is the city's greatest adventure.

name: Basilique Notre-Dame de Fourvière
description: The ornate white basilica crowning Lyon's hill of prayer, built after the Franco-Prussian War. The interior mosaics are breathtaking, and the terrace offers the finest view over both rivers.

name: Les Halles de Lyon Paul Bocuse
description: The sacred temple of Lyonnaise gastronomy — 48 stalls of cheese, charcuterie, praline tarts, and quenelles that represent the best of France's greatest food city. Named after the godfather of modern French cuisine.

name: Lyonnaise Bouchon Dinner
description: A bouchon is Lyon's answer to a local tavern — rustic, convivial, and dedicated to traditional dishes: tablier de sapeur (breaded tripe), quenelles de brochet, and tarte aux pralines. Book ahead.

name: Musée des Confluences
description: A stunning contemporary museum at the confluence of the Rhône and Saône rivers, housed in a spectacular deconstructivist steel-and-glass building. Natural history, civilisations, and society all under one roof.

name: Fushimi Inari Shrine
description: Thousands of vermilion torii gates wind up the forested mountain above Kyoto, creating one of Japan's most iconic and atmospheric experiences. Go at dusk or dawn to avoid the crowds.

name: Kinkaku-ji (Golden Pavilion)
description: The three-storey Zen temple pavilion covered in gold leaf, reflected perfectly in Mirror Pond. The Japanese garden surrounding it is a masterpiece of Muromachi-period design.

name: Arashiyama Bamboo Grove
description: A towering forest of Moso bamboo that creates an otherworldly green tunnel of sound and light. Combine with the Tenryu-ji Zen garden and a boat ride on the Hozu River.

name: Higashiyama Historic District
description: The best-preserved historic streetscape in Japan — stone-paved lanes lined with wooden machiya townhouses, temples, and craft shops lead through Gion to Kiyomizu-dera temple.

name: Philosopher's Path
description: A 2km canal-side walk through Higashiyama lined with hundreds of cherry trees. Named after philosopher Nishida Kitaro who meditated here daily. In spring the sakura tunnel is incomparable.

name: Nishiki Market
description: Kyoto's 'Kitchen' — a covered arcade with over 100 stalls selling tofu, tsukemono pickles, matcha confections, fresh fish, and Kyoto-style obanzai cuisine. Essential for understanding local food culture.

name: Gion Geisha District
description: Kyoto's most famous geisha district, where maiko (apprentice geishas) hurry between ochaya teahouses in the evening. Hanamikoji Street is the most atmospheric, lined with traditional ochaya.

name: Traditional Tea Ceremony
description: Participating in a formal chado tea ceremony in an old machiya gives profound insight into Japanese aesthetics — the precise choreography, the wabi-sabi ceramics, the bowl of bitter matcha.

name: Kaiseki at Kikunoi
description: A three-Michelin-star kaiseki restaurant presenting Kyoto cuisine across a dozen courses tied to the season. Each dish is a work of art — edible proof that Japanese cooking is the world's most refined.

name: Kyoto Kissaten Coffee Culture
description: Kyoto is famous for its old-fashioned kissaten (coffee shops) that have survived since the 1950s — dark wood interiors, siphon brewing, buttered toast, and absolute silence. Omotesando Koffee is the modern heir.

name: Kurama Onsen
description: A mountain hot-spring bath 45 minutes from Kyoto centre, reached through cedar forests. The outdoor rotenburo bath allows you to soak while looking up at the mountain canopy — transformative.

name: Dotonbori Canal & Glico Man
description: Osaka's neon-lit entertainment district, centred on a canal whose banks are lined with restaurants, takoyaki stalls, and the iconic Glico Running Man LED billboard. The city's energy distilled into one street.

name: Osaka Castle
description: The magnificent 16th-century castle of Toyotomi Hideyoshi, surrounded by a vast park. The interior museum traces the castle's turbulent history, while the top floor commands views across the city.

name: Kuromon Ichiba Market
description: Osaka's 'Kitchen' — a covered market with over 180 stalls of fresh fish, Wagyu beef, Osaka street food, and produce. Eating while walking is mandatory; try the fugu sashimi and live oysters.

name: Takoyaki & Okonomiyaki
description: Osaka is the home of takoyaki (octopus balls in a dashi batter) and okonomiyaki (savoury pancake). Fugetsu in Namba is the definitive address — the batter recipe hasn't changed in 70 years.

name: Shinsekai Retro District
description: A nostalgic neighbourhood built in 1912 modelled on Paris and New York, now famous for kushikatsu (skewered, breaded meats and vegetables) restaurants and the Tsutenkaku Tower.

name: Spa World
description: A vast 11-storey spa resort in Shinsekai with European and Asian bathing zones, outdoor rotenburo baths, and a water park. An authentic slice of Japanese bath culture.

name: Museo del Prado
description: Spain's national museum is among the world's greatest — Velázquez's Las Meninas, Goya's dark paintings, Bosch's Garden of Earthly Delights, and El Greco all in a single building. Plan for half a day.

name: Museo Reina Sofía
description: Spain's national modern art museum, home to Picasso's Guernica — a painting so powerful it can stop you in your tracks. Dalí and Miró are also exceptionally well represented.

name: Museo Thyssen-Bornemisza
description: The third vertex of Madrid's Golden Triangle of Art, tracing Western painting from the 13th century to Pop Art. The concentration of masterworks per square meter is extraordinary.

name: Palacio Real
description: The official residence of the Spanish Royal Family, with 3,418 rooms it is the largest royal palace in Western Europe by floor area. The Royal Armory and Banquet Hall are the highlights.

name: Parque del Retiro
description: Madrid's great green lung — 350 acres of formal gardens, a boating lake, the Crystal Palace, and the Velázquez Palace. Sunday mornings here are quintessentially Madrileño.

name: Mercado de San Miguel
description: A beautiful 1916 iron-and-glass market offering some of Madrid's finest pintxos, jamón ibérico, oysters, and vermouth. Standing and grazing is the correct approach.

name: Malasaña & Chueca Nightlife
description: Madrid's nightlife is legendary — dinner at 10pm, clubs at 2am, closing at 6am. Malasaña is bohemian indie bars, Chueca is LGBTQ+ friendly chic, La Latina is flamenco and old Madrid.

name: El Rastro Sunday Market
description: Spain's most famous flea market, held every Sunday in La Latina. Over 3,500 stalls sell antiques, vintage clothing, art, and curiosities across streets that fill with half of Madrid.

name: Gran Vía Walk
description: Madrid's Broadway — the grand early 20th-century boulevard lined with Beaux-Arts buildings, cinemas, shops, and cafés. The Telefónica Building and Metropolis Building are architectural highlights.

name: Real Alcázar de Sevilla
description: The oldest royal palace still in use in Europe, a masterpiece of Mudéjar architecture with intricately tiled halls, horseshoe arches, and gardens modelled on Islamic paradise gardens. Filmed as Dorne in Game of Thrones.

name: Seville Cathedral & La Giralda
description: The world's largest Gothic cathedral, built on the site of a Moorish mosque — the Giralda minaret was converted into a bell tower. Columbus is buried here in an elaborate tomb.

name: Triana Barrio
description: The flamenco heartland across the Guadalquivir from central Seville — birthplace of flamenco legends, home to bullfighting craftsmen, and lined with ceramic-tiled tapas bars. Authentic Seville lives here.

name: Live Flamenco Show
description: Seville is the birthplace of flamenco — seeing a genuine tablao performance, with the percussive footwork, wailing cante jondo, and fierce guitar, is among Spain's most visceral cultural experiences.

name: Tapas at El Rinconcillo
description: Spain's oldest tapas bar, continuously open since 1670. The chalked-up tabs, hanging jamones, and century-old tiles create the most authentic tapas experience in Seville — order spinach with chickpeas.

name: Marienplatz & Rathaus-Glockenspiel
description: The heart of Munich since 1158, flanked by the neo-Gothic Neues Rathaus with its famous Glockenspiel carillon that performs daily. The Frauenkirche's twin towers are the city's defining skyline.

name: Englischer Garten
description: Larger than Central Park, Munich's green heart has a river wave where surfers ride year-round, a Japanese tea house, beer gardens, and the Chinesischer Turm pagoda tower.

name: Deutsches Museum
description: The world's largest science and technology museum with 73,000 objects across 80 departments. The planetarium, chemistry lab, and underground mining section are standouts among its 17km of corridors.

name: Hofbräuhaus am Platzl
description: The world's most famous beer hall, founded in 1589 as the Royal Court Brewery. Sitting at long communal tables in the vaulted hall, drinking from litre Masskrug steins to oompah music, is Munich in a nutshell.

name: Viktualienmarkt
description: A 200-year-old daily farmers' market in the city centre, with 140 stalls of Bavarian cheeses, sausages, Obatzda, and seasonal produce. The central beer garden under maypole is perfect for lunch.

name: Nymphenburg Palace
description: The summer residence of Bavarian royalty, set in formal French gardens with canals, cascades, and hunting lodges. The Gallery of Beauties — 36 portraits commissioned by Ludwig I — is the highlight indoors.

name: Alte Pinakothek
description: One of the oldest and most important art galleries in the world, with a collection covering European painting from the 14th to 18th centuries — Rubens, Dürer, Rembrandt, and Raphael all in one building.

name: Theresienwiese (Oktoberfest Grounds)
description: Even outside October, the Theresienwiese meadow is a pilgrimage site for beer culture enthusiasts. Visit the bronze statue of Bavaria and the small permanent fairground that remains year-round.

name: Speicherstadt Warehouse District
description: A UNESCO World Heritage site — the world's largest warehouse complex, built on oak piles in the Elbe harbour from 1883. The brick neo-Gothic architecture, canals, and converted cultural spaces are extraordinary.

name: Elbphilharmonie
description: Herzog & de Meuron's glass wave atop an old warehouse is the world's most celebrated new concert hall. Even if you don't attend a concert, the free public Plaza on the 8th floor has Hamburg's best panorama.

name: Reeperbahn & St. Pauli
description: Hamburg's legendary red-light and entertainment district, where the Beatles played their first gigs in 1960. Today it's the heart of Hamburg's nightlife — music venues, bars, and clubs that never close.

name: Sunday Fischmarkt
description: Every Sunday from 5am (7am in winter), Hamburg's most famous institution — the fish market by the Elbe with auctioneers bellowing at the stalls, live music, and the smell of smoked eel and fresh buns.

name: Alster Lakes
description: The inner and outer Alster lakes in the city centre create a remarkable urban water landscape. Hire a kayak, take a steamboat, or simply walk the 7km promenade as Hamburgers do on weekends.

name: Miniatur Wunderland
description: The world's largest model railway exhibition, with 16km of track and 10,000 trains in meticulously detailed scenes from Hamburg, Scandinavia, the USA, and the airport — with a real functioning mini-airport.

name: Schanzenviertel
description: Hamburg's most creative neighbourhood — independent boutiques, concept stores, vegetarian cafés, and street art fill the streets between Schulterblatt and the Rote Flora squat. The city's Brooklyn.
